title: How to Think Like Socrates: Ancient Philosophy as a Way of Life in the Modern World
author: Donald J. Robertson

chapters.title: It's OK not to know
chapters.text:
Socrates never wrote a single book, but he knew how to ask the right questions. The three pillars of his wisdom — challenging assumptions, embracing uncertainty, and seeking truth — were proclaimed so radical they cost him his life. Thousands of years later, his ideas are as powerful as they were then.

True knowledge begins not with certainty but with the courage to admit what you don't know.

Donald J. Robertson realized we may need Socrates' wisdom in these challenging times. So, if we don't want to lose our minds to anxiety and irrational fears or want to improve our everyday routine (unheard of in philosophy!), we can give the man a chance.

If you wonder how philosophy is connected to practical matters, stick around to find out. By the time you reach the end, you'll have a step-by-step guide to start thinking like Socrates today — no lush beard required.

chapters.title: Life is our personal trial
chapters.text:
The year is 399 BCE. Socrates is barefoot, standing before hundreds of Athenians, and ready to advocate for his life. In his seventies, accused of impiety and corrupting the youth, he started his speech before corrupt jurors by calling himself wise. Once, his lifelong friend Chaerephon asked the Oracle of Delphi if there was someone wiser than him. The Oracle named Socrates the wisest man; the crowd lamented more.

Socrates, who believed you must question what you learn, went on to speak to the wisest people in Athens, only to discover they were the most foolish ones. Here's the lesson from this: the Socratic method requires you to think actively. When we read or listen to new information, we receive it passively, without questioning.

Instead, Socrates asked us to filter everything we consume through our knowledge and values and test it, as he did with the Oracle's words. The crowd assumed he was arrogant. More than this, they were angry with his straight gaze, feeling like they were the ones prosecuted.

Philosophy is learned through practice, not by memorizing books. To think is to live.

In his search, Socrates eventually concluded that true wisdom comes from realizing we cannot know everything. Foolishness comes from thinking that everything we know is all there is to know. However, by thinking so, we refuse alternative experiences and ways of living. If we do that, we reject the understanding that is different from ours, thus further limiting our knowledge. And that is foolish, according to Socrates.

The philosopher warned his persecutors from taking a life unjustly because the weight of that decision would be on their shoulders. Socrates accepted his mortality a long time ago, so sparing his life in exchange for leaving philosophy was unacceptable to him. Either way, his life as a person and philosopher would end, and the end of this story is famous — the jury found Socrates guilty. The following day, he drank poison and died.

If you want to test the quality of your time and wisdom, imagine yourself on trial, like Socrates, and try answering these questions:

• What did you spend most of your time doing?
• What did you invest your time in yesterday and the days before?
• How important were these activities?

Did you know? There is a whole word for people who love Greece and Greek culture — “philhellene.”

chapters.title: Wisdom is not given; it’s earned
chapters.text:
Socrates had been asking questions since he learned to speak. Whether true or not, the man's exceptional curiosity led him beyond the traditional understanding of philosophy. While most thinkers of his time were musing on the high matter, Socrates was more interested in the human mind. As time showed, he was onto the traces of modern therapy.

In simple terms, the Socratic method is a collection of written exercises designed to activate the part of us that bears knowledge. Rationality can be a close synonym for that. But why is knowledge so crucial to Socrates? Let's look at it with an example.

Euthydemus was a young man preoccupied with self-improvement by amassing wise men's writings. He believed that reading them made him a better person. Today, he would be called a "self-help junkie."

When Socrates met him, he asked Euthydemus questions like: "Is lying bad?" Of course, you may think. However, is it just or unjust to lie to a friend who is depressed when you know some news may hurt them more? If you stopped for a second to think, so did Euthydemus.

Self-help books may sometimes be useful, but they can't replace thinking for ourselves.

Testing and challenging what you consume can help avoid manipulation.

In many cases, it often involves going against our fear. People naturally avoid things that scare them, which applies to both courageous and fearful examples. What differentiates them is that brave people know that a bit of pain today prevents greater pain tomorrow, so they act despite fear. Scared people lack this knowledge; the opposite of knowing is ignorance. Ignorance hides in generalized scenarios, sometimes found in self-help books. Exercising the Socratic method can help us realize that life is more nuanced than it seems. For this, you can do the following:

• Make two columns on a page. Label the first column with a positive virtue (e.g., justice) and the second column with a negative one (e.g., injustice).
• Start with the second column and write down unfair things. For example, stealing or killing is clearly bad.
• Now, use your creative thinking and identify situations when doing these unjust things can be acceptable.
• Try practicing with other virtues. Eventually, this method will help you see real situations in more than just black and white.

Through self-knowledge … men come to much good in life, and through self-deceit, they come to much harm. ~ Socrates

chapters.title: Love is all you ever need
chapters.text:
When Socrates was a young man, his father, Sophroniscus, was a talented sculptor who often employed his son to help. When young Socrates was carving the statues of three goddesses named Graces, he asked himself about the gratitude and generosity they represented. His concerns boiled down to one simple question: "What is virtue?"

Socrates was hungry for answers that no contemporary philosopher had. Even Anaxagoras, the greatest thinker of his youth, had disappointed him: the man explained his feelings or thoughts with what would be considered biology and physics today. While scientifically progressive then, Anaxagoras missed something critical that Socrates couldn't put his finger on — something sensible.

Luckily, Socrates owed his thirst for knowledge to his mother, Phaenarete. She pointed out he was looking for answers in the wrong place, or rather, the wrong sex. A professional matchmaker, she prepared young women to marry wealthy men, possessing skills bordering diplomacy and seduction.

Phaenarete introduced her son to the rarest person in Athens — a female philosopher or lover of wisdom. Ancient Greece wasn't famous for feminism, so it didn't occur to Socrates to talk to a woman. However, Aspasia — the philosopher — opened his eyes to the ultimate virtue male philosophers overlooked — love.

Men are not less empathetic naturally; society teaches them to restrict their emotions.

Aspasia talked about the following:

• Physical attraction wanes over time as a person's beauty fades.
• However, if you value a person's wisdom and character, your love will strengthen as they develop.
• In the same way, if you want to be loved genuinely, build your character since wise people detect superficiality.

As you can see, genuine love can positively influence our character. Furthermore, loving something in a person means desiring it for ourselves. Therefore, love is wanting something we are not or do not possess, usually something good. If we choose to pursue it, we follow love — for ourselves and those we want to be with, either romantically or as friends. And if love is betterment, then improving is becoming fulfilled through good deeds and wisdom. The Greeks even have a word for this — eudaimonia, genuine fulfillment.

"But how do I practice it?" That's a good question. Stay with us and try the exercise at the end.

chapters.title: The secret power of words
chapters.text:
Who would you choose — a nervous person with the right message or a great talker beating around the bush? No matter how professional the first person is, if they are not persuasive, the second person will win the audience without needing to be an expert. Socrates came to this conclusion after talking to Gorgias — a public speaking, or oratory, teacher known all over Athens.

Gorgias argued that oratory is knowing how to speak well on any subject, and Socrates noted that every expert must speak excellently about their craft. So, what makes the difference?

It's impossible to be an expert in everything, or it's no longer expertise. Therefore, a well-spoken person with no particular expertise uses their skills to delude people into believing they practice what they preach. Let's use the example from Socrates and Gorgias' debate, where they argued about teaching justice. While a philosopher can teach a person to know what is just, an orator can persuade them to believe what they mean by just.

According to Socrates, those who fall for loud words risk being ignorant. At the same time, Gorgias teaches the ignorant how to appear more persuasive than experts before an equally clueless audience. In the end, no one learns anything, and real experts remain unheard.

This scenario is especially popular in politics, where a speaker takes advantage of citizens' lack of knowledge to promote a policy that benefits them. It's not to say that public speaking is evil, but it does warn you to stay alert.

Emotions can distort your true thoughts; do your best to stay calm and avoid manipulation.

One way to do that is cognitive distancing, a technique for sorting your thoughts by observing them as if you were another person. Here's how:
1. Recall a recent conflict you had. What was it about? What did you say? How did you feel?
2. Write down negative automatic thoughts that come to your mind.
3. Now, sort each thought into two piles: "I believe that..." and "I know that..."
4. You can advance the sorting by adding the third person: "[Your name] believes\knows that..." Doing so will help you be more objective.

Practice this exercise regularly to easily separate your subjective thoughts from facts and notice when others try to manipulate you with their beliefs.

chapters.title: Where's the evidence?
chapters.text:
Separating things you know are facts and those you believe to be true can be confusing at first. You may think: "How do I tell these meanings apart?" Let’s dive deeper into it by looking at an unexpected example — death.

But first, take notice of how you speak. We know that manipulative people are great at serving us a twisted opinion as truth. However, what may be surprising is that we can do the same to ourselves!

"That neighbor's dog always barks only at me!" or "You never help me around the house!" are examples of overstating trivial situations. These exaggerations are called "overgeneralizations," and they're one of four common cognitive distortions:

• Selective thinking means cherry-picking information outside the context. For example, a person may not remember good comments but will recite critical ones.
• Jumping to conclusions means making baseless assumptions. "Mind reading" is a good example of how we make assumptions about what people are thinking (especially about us).
• Exaggerating means blowing issues out of proportion and thinking in black-and-white categories. For example, believing someone can be only a friend or an enemy is exaggerating.

We often inadvertently engage in these cognitive distortions despite our beliefs or knowledge. As a result, we may start fearing things without knowing much about them. Take death, for example.

We are not disturbed by events; our opinions about them are disturbing.

Many of us know little about death, yet we believe it's terrifying. Socrates argues that this fear is based on our opinion, not facts. As long as we realize this distinction, it becomes easier to practice the cognitive distancing mentioned earlier. This, in return, helps avoid unnecessary anxiety and approach any doubts more confidently.

Robertson has a name for practicing your "cognitive distancing stamina" like Socrates — rational measurement. This practice aims to replace irrational fears with rational explanations. One way to make use of this exercise is to:

• Make two columns and name them "Short-term consequences" and "Long-term consequences."
• Take a situation or idea that makes you anxious and consider the consequences of holding on to it. The purpose is to realize that short-term discomfort is less painful than long-term overthinking. For example, fear of death keeps you from living a more fulfilling life.

chapters.title: Be just and receive justice
chapters.text:
We commonly associate Athens with democracy, yet Socrates lived to see his city become an oligarchy.

From 415 to 413 BCE, the Sicilian Expedition was a grave military disaster for Athens during the Peloponnesian War. The public temperature in the city-state was rising while oligarchic powers, known as Thirty Tyrants, were unfolding. The group was led by Critias, a political leader and Socrates' student. The teacher tried to warn his pupil, whose views on authority bordered on tyranny, from seizing power. Although the thinker failed to avert Critias from taking a wrong step, we find an important lesson in questioning our own perspective.

Socrates said that a good man accepts punishment for his wrongdoings with dignity. However, when a person feels they have been wronged, whether justly or not, they will react angrily. While anger is natural, it can lead the person overboard, especially if they prioritize themselves over the objective truth.

In cognitive therapy, anger is often a response to feeling threatened. The following anxiety can sometimes blur our perspective on what really happened. How often do we find ourselves wanting to avenge someone who made us feel terrible, especially if we admitted to the critique deep down?

Accepting critique now saves you from making more mistakes in the future.

If you find it difficult to get past your emotions and to the core of the issue that made you angry, Robertson suggests this exercise:

• Draw two columns headed "Evidence for" and "Evidence against."
• Take one of your core beliefs about injustice, for example, "People must treat me with respect!" and recall how you feel when you're disrespected. Then, spend some time unearthing other beliefs.
• List the objective evidence for and against your beliefs.
• Come up with alternative beliefs about the same situation. For instance, "I'd prefer if people treat me with respect, but if they don't, it doesn't mean other people won't."

The point of this exercise is to help you skip the emotional part and respond to unjust situations with wisdom.

Don't you see that admitting your mistakes, for the health of your own soul, is like swallowing bitter medicine for the good of your body? ~ Socrates

chapters.title: Try this
chapters.text:
Socrates didn’t just talk about philosophy — he lived it. Incorporate his wisdom into your daily routine and note any changes that occur.

• Question everything: Challenge assumptions — your own and others’. The next time you hear a strong claim, ask, “How do we know this is true?” or “What evidence supports this?”
• Practice intellectual humility: Each day, identify one thing you might be wrong about and explore alternative viewpoints with an open mind. Curiosity can take a person far.
• Engage in Socratic dialogue: Choose a thought-provoking question (such as "What is justice?” or "What makes a good life?") and discuss it with a friend. Don't try to ‘win' the argument, but to gain wisdom.
• Examine your beliefs: Write down five core beliefs about life, morality, or success. Then, for each one, ask: “Why do I believe this?” and "Could the opposite be true?”
• Embrace productive ignorance: Instead of feeling embarrassed when you don't know something, use it as an opportunity to learn more. Say, "I don't know, but I'd like to find out," and research a topic outside your usual interests.

Let yourself be inspired by things you've yet to discover, especially your beautiful mind.

Read it till the end?
